title: The Ghost that Jim Saw
content:
آ آ آ آ Why, as to that, said the engineer,
آ آ آ آ Ghosts ainï؟½t things we are apt to fear;
آ آ آ آ Spirits donï؟½t fool with levers much,
آ آ آ آ And throttle-valves donï؟½t take to such;
آ آ آ آ And as for Jim,
آ آ آ آ What happened to him
آ آ آ آ Was one half fact, and tï؟½other half whim!

آ آ آ آ Running one night on the line, he saw
آ آ آ آ A house as plain as the moral law
آ آ آ آ Just by the moonlit bank, and thence
آ آ آ آ Came a drunken man with no more sense
آ آ آ آ Than to drop on the rail
آ آ آ آ Flat as a flail,
آ آ آ آ As Jim drove by with the midnight mail.

آ آ آ آ Down went the patents steam reversed.
آ آ آ آ Too late! for there came a ï؟½thud.ï؟½ Jim cursed
آ آ آ آ As the fireman, there in the cab with him,
آ آ آ آ Kinder stared in the face of Jim,
آ آ آ آ And says, ï؟½What now?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Says Jim, ï؟½What now!
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve just run over a man, thatï؟½s how!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The fireman stared at Jim. They ran
آ آ آ آ Back, but they never found house nor man,
آ آ آ آ Nary a shadow within a mile.
آ آ آ آ Jim turned pale, but he tried to smile,
آ آ آ آ Then on he tore
آ آ آ آ Ten mile or more,
آ آ آ آ In quicker time than heï؟½d made afore.

آ آ آ آ Would you believe it! the very next night
آ آ آ آ Up rose that house in the moonlight white,
آ آ آ آ Out comes the chap and drops as before,
آ آ آ آ Down goes the brake and the rest encore;
آ آ آ آ And so, in fact,
آ آ آ آ Each night that act
آ آ آ آ Occurred, till folks swore Jim was cracked.

آ آ آ آ Humph! let me see; itï؟½s a year now, ï؟½most,
آ آ آ آ That I met Jim, East, and says, ï؟½Howï؟½s your ghost?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gone,ï؟½ says Jim; ï؟½and more, itï؟½s plain
آ آ آ آ That ghost donï؟½t trouble me again.
آ آ آ آ I thought I shook
آ آ آ آ That ghost when I took
آ آ آ آ A place on an Eastern line, but look!

آ آ آ آ ï؟½What should I meet, the first trip out,
آ آ آ آ But the very house we talked about,
آ آ آ آ And the selfsame man! ï؟½Well,ï؟½ says I, ï؟½I guess
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s time to stop this ï؟½yer foolishness.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ So I crammed on steam,
آ آ آ آ When there came a scream
آ آ آ آ From my fireman, that jest broke my dream:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½ï؟½Youï؟½ve killed somebody!ï؟½ Says I, ï؟½Not much!
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve been thar often, and thar ainï؟½t no such,
آ آ آ آ And now Iï؟½ll prove it!ï؟½ Back we ran,
آ آ آ آ And darn my skin! but thar was a man
آ آ آ آ On the rail, dead,
آ آ آ آ Smashed in the head!
آ آ آ آ Now I call that meanness!ï؟½ Thatï؟½s all Jim said.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
